comments:
Summary
This paper addresses detection of singleton review spammers by exploiting semantic similarity (WordNet-based) and topic-distribution similarity (LDA), including a bag-of-opinion-phrases variant. The motivation is clear: behavioral features are unavailable for one-time reviewers, so text-based methods are needed. The authors evaluate semantic and LDA-based detectors on three datasets (Yelp filtered/unfiltered, Trustpilot labeled data, and the Ott AMT dataset), compare against cosine baselines with POS and lemmatization variants, and report precision/recall/F1 across similarity thresholds and topic counts.

Strengths
- The focus on singleton reviewers fills an understudied practical gap: many reviewers post only once and cannot be profiled behaviorally.
- The work compares a knowledge-based semantic similarity (Mihalcea/WordNet extension) to reasonable vector-space baselines (cosine with POS/lemmatization), and explores two LDA formulations including opinion-phrase-based topics. The experimental scope (multiple datasets, thresholds, topic counts) is substantial.
- Results show semantic similarity can outperform simple cosine baselines for some thresholds/datasets, and the bag-of-opinion-phrases LDA variant shows promise in increasing aspect-level granularity.

Weaknesses and concerns
- Ground-truth labeling: the Yelp evaluation treats the platform’s “not recommended” (filtered) reviews as spam and “recommended” as truthful. The paper acknowledges prior works using this assumption but does not sufficiently discuss the limitations or potential label noise introduced by this heuristic. Similarly, Trustpilot labels are assumed correct because they come from the company; this assumption merits more scrutiny and discussion of possible bias.
- Evaluation methodology: the detector is thresholded over pairwise similarity scores and results are reported primarily as precision/recall/F1 versus threshold. Important evaluation details are missing or under-discussed: how are performance averages computed across businesses (per-business averaging or global?), how many review pairs were scored, and what is the trade-off in terms of false positives per business? There is no reporting of ROC/AUC or aggregate statistics independent of threshold selection. No statistical significance testing is reported to support claims that one method outperforms another.
- Limited baselines: baselines are restricted to cosine variants and the Mihalcea semantic method; the paper does not evaluate hybrid approaches (e.g., combining text-based measures with any available lightweight behavioral cues where possible) or stronger text baselines (e.g., n-gram or stylistic features previously used in the literature the paper cites). This limits claims about practical superiority.
- Practicality and scalability: no runtime or complexity analysis is provided. The WordNet-based pairwise semantic similarity and pairwise LDA-distribution comparisons can be expensive at scale (pairwise over many reviews per business); the paper does not discuss computational cost or techniques to scale to real production workloads.
- Sensitivity and hyperparameters: LDA topic counts and frequency filtering thresholds were chosen (10,30,50,70,100 topics; frequency cutoffs) but rationale and sensitivity analysis are limited. The paper notes some datasets respond better to particular topic counts, but it does not offer a principled way to choose them.
- Presentation issues: the provided draft contains numerous repeated sentences/paragraphs (likely formatting/artifact issues) that distract and should be cleaned. Some algorithmic details (e.g., exact computation of maxSim, which of the six word-to-word measures were used, handling out-of-vocabulary words) are only cursorily described and could be made more explicit.

Suggestions for improvement
- Strengthen discussion of dataset labeling: provide an analysis of label noise (sample inspection), or complement Yelp labels with a small human-annotated validation set to quantify the reliability of the Yelp filter as ground truth.
- Add aggregate evaluation metrics (ROC/AUC), per-business false positive rates, and perform statistical significance testing when comparing methods.
- Report computational cost (time/memory) of the semantic pairwise method and LDA variants, and discuss scalability strategies (e.g., blocking, approximate similarity search) if this is to be considered for production.
- Include additional baselines or ablation studies: e.g., stylistic or simple n-gram features, and a hybrid model that combines semantic similarity with lightweight behavioral signals where available.
- Clarify preprocessing and algorithmic choices in more detail (e.g., exact word-to-word similarity measures combined, handling of words not in WordNet, choice rationale for frequency thresholds and minimum document length).
- Clean up repeated text and improve organization of results to make comparisons easier to follow.

Overall recommendation
This paper makes a useful contribution by focusing on singleton reviewers and exploring semantic similarity and opinion-phrase topic models for detection. The experiments are reasonably extensive and the idea that semantic relation modeling helps beyond simple cosine is supported by the reported results. However, concerns about evaluation (ground truth quality, missing aggregate metrics and statistical tests), scalability, and baseline breadth limit the strength of the claims. I recommend acceptance as a poster contribution after revision addressing the dataset-labeling discussion, evaluation robustness (aggregate metrics and significance), and clarity/scalability concerns.
